title: Ebru tulip pattern
subtitle: Creating the classic Turkish marbling tulip motif using drop placement and stylus strokes on a carrageenan size
excerpt: The tulip is the signature motif of Turkish ebru marbling. Alternating drops of light and dark paint are placed in a diagonal row on the size, then the stylus is drawn through them in a repeated up-down motion to pull each blob into a petal. The finished tulip form emerges from the interaction of the drops and the stylus drag, it cannot be drawn; it must be built from the size up.
sourceNotes: Mustafa Düzgünman, Principles of Ebru (traditional transmission, 20th century). Ebru is an oral and visual tradition. No single canonical modern text; the tulip pattern is documented in multiple museum catalogues of Ottoman art and in Nedim Sözen, Ebru (1992).

glossaryTerms.definition: The thickened water surface in a marbling tray, prepared with carrageenan. The viscosity of the size is what holds the paint drops in place rather than letting them sink, if the size is too thin, drops sink; if too thick, they sit in domed islands without spreading.

A drop of paint deposited onto the size from a dropper or brush held 2-3 cm above the surface. Ox-gall in the paint causes it to spread outward in a disc. Each drop displaces the drops around it; the final composition is built up by successive drops pushing each other around the surface.

The distortion of the paint surface caused by drawing a pointed tool through the size. The stylus drags paint in the direction of travel and leaves paint from the disturbed surface on either side. A single drag through a drop produces a teardrop; two drags in opposite directions through a row of drops produce a petal.

content.text: : carrageenan mixed the day before use and allowed to settle and de-gas. Fresh carrageenan holds too many bubbles; it traps air under dropped paint and causes eruptions. Prepare 3-5 litres at 1.5 tsp carrageenan per litre and leave overnight in the tray before starting work.
Add 2-3 drops of ox-gall to each 30 ml of liquid acrylic and stir to combine. Test a single

on the size: it should spread to a disc 5-8 cm in diameter within a few seconds. Too little spreading means too little ox-gall; too much spreading means the size is too thin. Skim the surface between drops with a strip of newspaper to remove any residue before the next colour.

Drop a column of seven to nine alternating blossoms diagonally across the tray, light colour, dark colour, light, dark, spaced about 3 cm apart. The drops will push against each other; allow them to settle for 10 seconds before continuing.
Using the stylus end of the ebru tool, perform the
: starting above the top drop, pull through the centre of the column in one smooth stroke to the bottom. This creates the central stamen line of the tulip.

Return to the top and make a second stroke parallel to the first, 1.5 cm to the right. The paint from the first drag distorts into petals as the second stroke disturbs it. A third stroke 1.5 cm to the left of the first completes the three-petal tulip silhouette.
Drop a stem: add a thin line of green paint just below the flower using the dropper, then pull the stylus straight down from the flower base to drag a stem from the green drop.
Lower the alum-mordanted sheet face-down onto the size, let it make contact for 30 seconds, then peel from one corner. Rinse under cold water and hang to dry.

items.symptom: The drops sink below the surface rather than spreading
items.cause: The size is too thin or has lost viscosity through use.
items.fix: Skim the surface, drain the tray, and add a small amount of fresh carrageenan concentrate. Test again with a single drop before placing the tulip pattern.

items.symptom: The stylus drags produce jagged, irregular lines rather than smooth curves
items.cause: The stylus is being moved too quickly, or the paint has dried a skim on the surface.
items.fix: Move the stylus in a steady, unhurried motion at about 5 cm per second. Skim the surface with a strip of newspaper before each design to remove any dried paint residue.
